1:
"Now these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote, and possessed their land on the other side Jordan toward the rising of the sun, from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon, and all the plain on the east:" The very brief verse opens the record of the kings smitten east of Jordan. The grammatical voices note the record. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote, and possessed their land on the other side Jordan toward the rising of the sun, from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon" — the chapter records the kings defeated and the lands possessed east of Jordan; the now these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote (the kings smitten); the and possessed their land on the other side Jordan toward the rising of the sun, from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon, and all the plain on the east (the lands possessed east of Jordan); the figure (the record of the victories, the kings smitten and the lands possessed, the memorial of God's works; the truth that the conquests were recorded as a witness of God's faithfulness; the figure of the remembrance of God's mighty works, the victories told, "I will remember the works of the LORD," Psalm 77:11; the kings smitten east of Jordan, the inheritance possessed; the record of the conquest, vv.7). The verse holds together the record of the eastern kings.

On "Now these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote," the witnesses note the kings. Gill notes the kings smitten by Israel recorded (the record, Psalm 77:11) — the kings (smitten).

On "and possessed their land on the other side Jordan toward the rising of the sun, from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon, and all the plain on the east," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the lands: "possessed their land on the other side Jordan... from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon" — the lands possessed east of Jordan (the inheritance); the figure (the remembrance of God's mighty works); the providence (the record, Sihon king of the Amorites to follow, vv.2). The verse displays the record of the kings smitten east of Jordan.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the record — "these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote, and possessed their land on the other side Jordan," the record (Keil, Gill); "Now these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote," the kings (Psalm 77:11, Gill); "and possessed their land on the other side Jordan toward the rising of the sun, from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon, and all the plain on the east," the lands (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the record. The verse marks the kings: smitten. The verse displays the lands: possessed. The verse displays the east: of Jordan. The verse displays the conquest: recorded. The record displays the remembrance of God's mighty works. The verse displays the victories: told. The verse displays the inheritance: possessed. The verse displays the witness: kept. The record of the kings smitten east of Jordan displays the remembrance of God's works: these are the kings of the land, which the children of Israel smote, and possessed their land on the other side Jordan toward the rising of the sun, from the river Arnon unto mount Hermon (Psalm 77:11) — the record of the kings defeated and the lands possessed east of Jordan, the memorial of God's faithfulness in giving the inheritance. The verse displays Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon (vv.2-3).
2:
"Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon, and ruled from Aroer, which is upon the bank of the river Arnon, and from the middle of the river, and from half Gilead, even unto the river Jabbok, which is the border of the children of Ammon;" The very brief verse notes Sihon king of the Amorites and the extent of his realm. The grammatical voices note the king. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon, and ruled from Aroer... even unto the river Jabbok, which is the border of the children of Ammon" — Sihon, the Amorite king of Heshbon, ruled a wide realm east of Jordan; the Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon (Sihon of Heshbon); the and ruled from Aroer, which is upon the bank of the river Arnon, and from the middle of the river, and from half Gilead, even unto the river Jabbok (the extent of his realm); the figure (the great king Sihon, who refused Israel passage and fell, his realm given to Israel; the truth that Sihon, the first great foe east of Jordan, was smitten and his land possessed; the figure of the proud king brought low and his realm given to God's people, "Sihon king of the Amorites... for his mercy endureth for ever," Psalm 136:19; the king and his realm taken; the first conquest east of Jordan, vv.1). The verse holds together Sihon's realm.

On "Sihon king of the Amorites, who dwelt in Heshbon," the witnesses note the king. Gill notes Sihon, the Amorite king of Heshbon (the king, Psalm 136:19) — the king (Sihon of Heshbon).

On "and ruled from Aroer, which is upon the bank of the river Arnon, and from the middle of the river, and from half Gilead, even unto the river Jabbok, which is the border of the children of Ammon," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the realm: "ruled from Aroer... even unto the river Jabbok" — the extent of Sihon's realm (the wide kingdom); the figure (the proud king brought low and his realm given); the providence (the realm, the plain to the sea of Chinneroth to follow, vv.3). The verse displays Sihon king of the Amorites and his realm.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays the plain to the sea of Chinneroth and the salt sea (vv.3).
3:
"And from the plain to the sea of Chinneroth on the east, and unto the sea of the plain, even the salt sea on the east, the way to Bethjeshimoth; and from the south, under Ashdothpisgah:" The very brief verse continues the bounds of Sihon's realm. The grammatical voices note the bounds. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "from the plain to the sea of Chinneroth on the east, and unto the sea of the plain, even the salt sea... the way to Bethjeshimoth; and from the south, under Ashdothpisgah" — the realm of Sihon reached from the sea of Chinneroth to the salt sea; the and from the plain to the sea of Chinneroth on the east (to the sea of Chinneroth); the and unto the sea of the plain, even the salt sea on the east, the way to Bethjeshimoth; and from the south, under Ashdothpisgah (to the salt sea); the figure (the bounds of the realm given to Israel, the wide inheritance; the truth that the whole realm of Sihon was possessed, the land given; the figure of the inheritance fully bounded and possessed, the gift complete; the bounds of the conquest east of Jordan; the realm given, vv.2). The verse holds together the bounds of Sihon's realm.

On "And from the plain to the sea of Chinneroth on the east," the witnesses note the bound. Gill notes the realm reaching to the sea of Chinneroth (the bound) — the bound (to Chinneroth).

On "and unto the sea of the plain, even the salt sea on the east, the way to Bethjeshimoth; and from the south, under Ashdothpisgah," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the bounds: "unto the sea of the plain, even the salt sea... under Ashdothpisgah" — the realm to the salt sea (the inheritance); the figure (the inheritance fully bounded and possessed); the providence (the bounds, the realm of Og king of Bashan to follow, vv.4). The verse displays Og king of Bashan, of the remnant of the giants (vv.4-5).
4:
"And the coast of Og king of Bashan, which was of the remnant of the giants, that dwelt at Ashtaroth and at Edrei," The very brief verse notes Og king of Bashan, of the remnant of the giants. The grammatical voices note the giant king. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the coast of Og king of Bashan, which was of the remnant of the giants, that dwelt at Ashtaroth and at Edrei" — Og, the giant king of Bashan, was smitten and his land possessed; the and the coast of Og king of Bashan, which was of the remnant of the giants (Og of the giants); the that dwelt at Ashtaroth and at Edrei (his cities); the figure (the giant king Og, of the remnant of the Rephaim, smitten by Israel; the truth that even the giant king fell before God's people, his realm given; the figure of the great and terrible foe overcome by God's power, the giant brought low, "Og the king of Bashan: for his mercy endureth for ever," Psalm 136:20; the giant king fallen; the second great conquest east of Jordan, vv.2). The verse holds together Og the giant king.

On "And the coast of Og king of Bashan, which was of the remnant of the giants," the witnesses note the giant. Gill notes Og, of the remnant of the giants (the giant king, Psalm 136:20) — the giant (Og of Bashan).

The verse displays Og king of Bashan, of the remnant of the giants.

5:
"And reigned in mount Hermon, and in Salcah, and in all Bashan, unto the border of the Geshurites and the Maachathites, and half Gilead, the border of Sihon king of Heshbon." The very brief verse notes the extent of Og's realm over Bashan and Gilead. The grammatical voices note the realm. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "reigned in mount Hermon, and in Salcah, and in all Bashan... and half Gilead, the border of Sihon king of Heshbon" — Og's realm reached over Bashan to the border of Sihon's; the and reigned in mount Hermon, and in Salcah, and in all Bashan (Og's realm over Bashan); the unto the border of the Geshurites and the Maachathites, and half Gilead, the border of Sihon king of Heshbon (to the border of Sihon); the figure (the wide realm of Og given to Israel, the two kingdoms east of Jordan possessed; the truth that the realms of Sihon and Og together were given to Israel, the inheritance east of Jordan; the figure of the two great kingdoms overcome and given, the inheritance possessed; the realm of Og taken; the conquest east of Jordan complete, vv.4). The verse holds together Og's realm.

On "And reigned in mount Hermon, and in Salcah, and in all Bashan," the witnesses note the realm. Gill notes Og reigning over Bashan (the realm) — the realm (over Bashan).

On "unto the border of the Geshurites and the Maachathites, and half Gilead, the border of Sihon king of Heshbon," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the border: "unto the border of the Geshurites and the Maachathites, and half Gilead, the border of Sihon" — Og's realm to the border of Sihon's (the two realms); the figure (the two great kingdoms overcome and given); the providence (the realm, Moses giving the land to the two and a half tribes to follow, vv.6). The verse displays the extent of Og's realm.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays Moses giving the land to the Reubenites, Gadites, and half Manasseh (vv.6).
6:
"Them did Moses the servant of the LORD and the children of Israel smite: and Moses the servant of the LORD gave it for a possession unto the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh." The very brief verse notes Moses smiting these kings and giving the land to the two and a half tribes. The grammatical voices note the gift. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Moses the servant of the LORD and the children of Israel smite: and Moses... gave it for a possession unto the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh" — Moses smote Sihon and Og and gave their land to the eastern tribes; the them did Moses the servant of the LORD and the children of Israel smite (Moses smiting the kings); the and Moses the servant of the LORD gave it for a possession unto the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh (the land given to the eastern tribes); the figure (Moses, the servant of the LORD, giving the eastern inheritance, the conquest under Moses; the truth that the land east of Jordan was given by Moses to the two and a half tribes; the figure of the inheritance distributed by God's servant, the gift of the land; the eastern tribes settled; the conquest under Moses recorded, vv.1). The verse holds together the gift to the eastern tribes.

On "Them did Moses the servant of the LORD and the children of Israel smite," the witnesses note the conquest. Gill notes Moses smiting Sihon and Og (the conquest) — the conquest (Moses smote them).

On "and Moses the servant of the LORD gave it for a possession unto the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the gift: "Moses... gave it for a possession unto the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh" — the land given to the eastern tribes (the inheritance); the figure (the inheritance distributed by God's servant); the providence (the gift, the kings smitten west of Jordan to follow, vv.7). The verse displays Moses giving the land to the two and a half tribes.

The verse displays the kings whom Joshua smote on the west side of Jordan (vv.7).
7:
"And these are the kings of the country which Joshua and the children of Israel smote on this side Jordan on the west, from Baalgad in the valley of Lebanon even unto the mount Halak, that goeth up to Seir; which Joshua gave unto the tribes of Israel for a possession according to their divisions;" The witnesses note the record of the kings Joshua smote west of Jordan and the land given to the tribes — the crux of the faithfulness of God in giving his people the promised inheritance. On "these are the kings... which Joshua and the children of Israel smote on this side Jordan on the west... which Joshua gave unto the tribes of Israel for a possession," interpreters expound the record. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the inheritance given — "and these are the kings of the country which Joshua and the children of Israel smote on this side Jordan on the west, from Baalgad in the valley of Lebanon even unto the mount Halak... which Joshua gave unto the tribes of Israel for a possession according to their divisions": Joshua smote the kings west of Jordan and gave their land to the tribes for an inheritance; the witnesses note the significance — the faithfulness of God in giving his people the land he promised, the kings smitten and the inheritance distributed;the truth that the inheritance was God's gift, distributed to each tribe by lot, the land possessed as promised, "for ye shall inherit the land," Numbers 33:54; . The figure of the faithfulness of God in giving his people the promised inheritance.

On "and these are the kings of the country which Joshua and the children of Israel smote on this side Jordan on the west, from Baalgad in the valley of Lebanon even unto the mount Halak, that goeth up to Seir," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the kings smitten. Keil and Ellicott note the record of the kings Joshua smote from Lebanon to Seir, the whole land west of Jordan conquered — the kings (smitten west of Jordan).

On "which Joshua gave unto the tribes of Israel for a possession according to their divisions," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the inheritance. Calvin and Gill note the land given to the tribes by their divisions, the inheritance distributed as God promised, a figure of the eternal inheritance of the saints (the inheritance possessed, Numbers 33:54; 1 Peter 1:4) — the figure (the heavenly inheritance distributed to the saints); the providence (the inheritance, the list of the thirty-one kings to follow, vv.9-24). The verse displays the kings Joshua smote west of Jordan and the land given to the tribes.

The witnesses agree the substance: Joshua smote the kings west of Jordan and gave their land to the tribes for an inheritance as God promised (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of God's faithfulness in the inheritance: which Joshua gave unto the tribes for a possession, God keeping his covenant and failing in no good thing he had spoken (Joshua 21:43-45; Numbers 33:54) (1 Peter 1:4). The verse displays the inheritance-faithfulness crux. The kings Joshua smote west of Jordan and the land given to the tribes display the crux: these are the kings... which Joshua and the children of Israel smote on this side Jordan on the west... which Joshua gave unto the tribes of Israel for a possession (Joshua 21:43-45; Numbers 33:54; 1 Peter 1:4) — the faithfulness of God in giving his people the promised land, a figure of the eternal inheritance of the saints. The verse displays the list of the thirty-one kings, beginning with Jericho and Ai (vv.9-24).
8:
"In the mountains, and in the valleys, and in the plains, and in the springs, and in the wilderness, and in the south country; the Hittites, the Amorites, and the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites:" The very brief verse notes the regions and the nations whose kings were smitten. The grammatical voices note the regions. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "in the mountains, and in the valleys, and in the plains... the Hittites, the Amorites, and the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites" — the kings of every region and nation were smitten; the in the mountains, and in the valleys, and in the plains, and in the springs, and in the wilderness, and in the south country (every region); the the Hittites, the Amorites, and the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites (every nation); the figure (the kings of every region and nation conquered, the whole land subdued; the truth that all the regions and all the nations of Canaan fell before Israel; the figure of the complete victory over all the powers of the land, every nation overcome; the regions and nations subdued; the whole conquest summed, vv.7). The verse holds together the regions and nations.

On "In the mountains, and in the valleys, and in the plains, and in the springs, and in the wilderness, and in the south country," the witnesses note the regions. Gill notes the kings of every region smitten (the regions) — the regions (every quarter).

On "the Hittites, the Amorites, and the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the nations: "the Hittites, the Amorites, and the Canaanites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites" — every nation of Canaan smitten (the conquest); the figure (the complete victory over all the powers of the land); the providence (the nations, the list of the kings to follow, vv.9). The verse displays the regions and nations whose kings were smitten.

The verse displays the list of the kings, beginning with the king of Jericho (vv.9).
9:

Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the king of Jericho, one; the king of Ai, which is beside Bethel, one" — the list of conquered kings begins with Jericho and Ai, the first two cities taken; the the king of Jericho, one (Jericho first); the the king of Ai, which is beside Bethel, one (Ai second); the figure (the list of the conquered kings, each numbered, the memorial of God's victories; the truth that the first two cities taken head the roll of the conquest; the figure of the record that numbers God's mercies one by one, the victories told; the kings numbered, the conquest remembered; the roll of the kings, vv.7). The verse holds together the first kings.

10:

The verse holds together the southern kings.

"The king of Tirzah, one: all the kings thirty and one." The witnesses note the close of the list, the king of Tirzah and the sum of all the kings, thirty and one — the crux of the completeness of God's victory and the full record of his faithfulness. On "the king of Tirzah, one: all the kings thirty and one," interpreters expound the sum. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the full tally — "the king of Tirzah, one: all the kings thirty and one": the roll closes with Tirzah, and the whole number of conquered kings is summed, thirty and one; the witnesses note the significance; the lesson — that God gave his people a full and complete victory, every king numbered, none of his promises failing, the tally a witness that he did all he said, "there failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken unto the house of Israel; all came to pass," Joshua 21:45; the truth that God's works are to be numbered and remembered, the mercies told one by one, "who can utter the mighty acts of the LORD? who can shew forth all his praise?" Psalm 106:2; the figure — the full record of God's victories, the complete triumph over the powers arrayed against his people, a figure of the final and total victory of Christ over all his enemies, "he must reign, till he hath put all enemies under his feet," 1 Corinthians 15:25. The figure of the completeness of God's victory and the full record of his faithfulness.

On "the king of Tirzah, one," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the last king. Keil and Ellicott note the roll closing with the king of Tirzah, the last of the conquered kings named — the last king (Tirzah).

On "all the kings thirty and one," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the sum. Calvin and Gill note the whole number summed, thirty and one kings, the completeness of the conquest and of God's faithfulness, none of his promises failing, a figure of the final and total victory of Christ over all his foes (the complete victory, Joshua 21:45; 1 Corinthians 15:25) — the figure (the complete and final victory of God over the powers of evil); the providence (the sum, the division of the land to follow, ch.13). The verse displays the close of the list, the sum of all the kings, thirty and one.

The witnesses agree the substance: the roll closes with Tirzah and the whole number of conquered kings is summed, thirty and one (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the complete victory: all the kings thirty and one — the completeness of the conquest and of God's faithfulness, the full number of the kings subdued set down as a memorial, the whole power of Canaan broken, God failing in no good thing he had spoken (Joshua 21:45), his mighty acts to be numbered and remembered (Psalm 106:2), a figure of the final and total victory of Christ over all his enemies, who must reign till all are put under his feet (1 Corinthians 15:25). The verse displays the complete-victory crux. The close of the list, the sum of all the kings, thirty and one, displays the crux: the king of Tirzah, one: all the kings thirty and one (Joshua 21:45; Psalm 106:2; 1 Corinthians 15:25) — the completeness of the conquest and of God's faithfulness, the whole power of Canaan broken and every king numbered, a figure of the final and total victory of Christ over all his enemies. The verse closes the record of the conquest, before the division of the land among the tribes (ch.13).
